items.q: Asch's line study primarily demonstrates which type of conformity?
items.correct: Normative social influence (compliance to fit in).
items.misconception: Informational social influence (believing others know better).
items.why: Students assume participants genuinely believed the wrong line was correct. Asch's debriefs showed they knew it was wrong but conformed publicly to avoid rejection - this is normative, not informational.

items.correct: Learning by observing the consequences others receive for behaviour.
items.misconception: Being directly rewarded for copying someone.
items.why: Students confuse vicarious with direct reinforcement. 'Vicarious' means experienced through someone else - you see them rewarded/punished and adjust your own behaviour accordingly.

items.q: Why is high IQ a problem for the statistical infrequency definition?
items.correct: It is statistically rare but not abnormal/undesirable.
items.misconception: Because high IQ is actually common.
items.why: Students miss that statistical infrequency lacks a value judgement - it just measures rarity. High IQ is rare but desirable, exposing the definition's limitation.

items.q: On critical trials Asch's 1951 participants conformed to the wrong answer on roughly what percentage of trials?
items.why: It's 37%. “12%” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which type of conformity is deepest, with public and private acceptance?
items.why: It's Internalisation. “Compliance” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximately what percentage of Asch's participants conformed on at least one critical trial?
items.why: Exact answer: 75. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
